What Recession? It's a Depression
What's all this debate about whether a recession is coming or whether one has already begun? Don't worry. It's not a recession. It's a depression.

In my profession, bankruptcy law, we are seeing dozens of formerly wealthy real estate developers. Not long ago, we heard about a developer whose net worth previously had as many digits as his Social Security number. Now, his net worth is his Social Security number.

A Grass-Roots Crusade to Build a Library
AN avid reader, Lorrie Gorman grew up in a suburb of Seattle that had a spacious library with a large collection of books and reference materials, quiet reading rooms and varied activities. So when she and her husband went house-hunting in Pelham Manor, one question she asked was whether the municipality had a library.

"She said, 'Yes,' and I just accepted that," Mrs. Gorman recalled recently. "I guess you have certain expectations that what you knew as a child is what exists all over. Unfortunately, that's not the case."

Looking Ahead

Why the Trade Talks Fizzled
So the world trade talks in Brussels were a bust, and the wicked protectionist European Community is widely portrayed as responsible. Once again the Europeans lost the propaganda battle. The world is to be plunged into trade wars and recession because of a European addiction to subsidizing farmers. 

Here is one European's dissenting view. Two things went wrong. First, the timing turned sour. This was nobody's fault. The two previous General Agreement on Tariffs and Trade negotiations, the Kennedy round in the 1960's and the Tokyo round in the 1970's, were concluded when the economic outlook was bright. This time, the storm signals are out across the world. If a rising tide lifts all boats, a falling tide can deposit them on the mud at the bottom of the harbor.

NEW JERSEY GUIDE
SPORTSMEN'S SHOW 

 A 2,000-gallon glass-enclosed fishing pond where champion anglers will demonstrate their expertise will be the centerpiece of the Garden State Outdoor Sportsmen's Show, opening Thursday at the Raritan Center Exposition Hall in Edison.

More than 50 free seminars and clinics will be presented at the show as 400 exhibitors -- outfitters, guides, Federal and state agencies, fishing and hunting organizations and support services of all sorts for the sportsman -- display and demonstrate their wares and services.

The glass pond, officially designated a fishing simulator for the purposes of this show, belongs to the American Fishing Institute, a department of Indiana State University at Terre Haute, and is used for studying fish in the close approximation of their natural habitat and for courses in ecology. It is 34 feet long, 6 feet wide and 4 feet deep and will be stocked with bass to challenge the casting skills of expert fishermen, who will be available for consultation at the show.

Techniques used in hunting birds and small game with the assistance of a fine four-legged companion will also be demonstrated. Labradors will be featured in an exhibition of the disciplined help that an exemplary hunting dog provides for his master.

About 85 percent of the show will be devoted to fishing, with the rest dedicated to hunting and other outdoor activities.

The hours will be 1 to 10 P.M. Thursday and Friday, 10 A.M. to 10 P.M. Saturday and 10 A.M. to 6 P.M. next Sunday at the exposition hall on Sunfield Avenue, off Exit 129 South, or 127 North, of the Garden State Parkway, or Exit 10 of the New Jersey Turnpike. Admission is $7. Parking is free.

Information: (201) 417-1400.
KING COMMEMORATION 


The 62d anniversary of the birth of the Rev. Dr. Martin Luther King Jr. will be celebrated in song and dance by three Jersey City groups at commemoration ceremonies at 6 P.M. Tuesday in the rotunda of the Hudson County Justice William J. Brennan Jr. Courthouse in the city.

The 45-member Snyder High School Chorus, an award-winning group that has been invited to perform in the Washington observance celebrating the bicentennial of the day the Bill of Rights went into effect, the Hudson Repertory Dance Theater and the Spirit of Life, a jazz ensemble, will perform. 

Dr. Clement A. Price will deliver the keynote address, "Dr. King and the Making of American Society." Dr. Price is associate professor and acting chairman of the department of history at Rutgers University in Newark and co-chairman of the State of New Jersey Martin Luther King Jr. Commission. He is the author of "Freedom Not Far Distant: A Documentary History of Afro-Americans in New Jersey." 

Admission is free to the event at the courthouse, 583 Newark Avenue.  Information: (201) 915-1212.
'FROM BACH TO BOP' 

 Music "From Bach to Bop" will be played by the Four Winds, a quartet whose members each play four or five different instruments, at the New Philharmonic of New Jersey's Music for Singles concert next Sunday at 7 P.M. at the Madison Hotel on Route 24 in Convent Station. 

The program includes music from Mozart and Gershwin and an all-saxophone jam session of jazz improvisation.

The quartet is composed of William Shadel, first clarinetist for the Philharmonic and a founder of the New Jersey Chamber Music Society, Girard Mohen, Frank Santagata and Sue Terry.

After the concert, there will be a reception with cash bar for the audience to meet the musicians.

Tickets are $12 by mail (New Philharmonic, 19 Beach Place, Maplewood, N.J. 07040) or $15 at the door.  Information: (201) 762-8449.
ROCK MOVIE 

 "Mystery Train," Jim Jarmusch's 1989 feature film about legendary rock music pioneers who haunt the shabby Arcade Hotel in Memphis, will be shown at 8 P.M. on Thursday at the Clairidge Triple Cinema in Montclair.

The film was winner of the Best Artistic Contribution award at the 1989 Cannes Film Festival.

Two young Japanese tourists, an Italian widow and an unemployed drunk are among the pilgrims involved in the film, which was inspired by Chaucer's "Canterbury Tales." The film features the Elvis Presley songs "Mystery Train" and "Blue Moon," as well as songs by Roy Orbison, Otis Redding and Bobby Blue Bland. Tom Waits provides the voice of a disk jockey. Screamin' Jay Hawkins, the flamboyant blues singer, is one of the actors.

The film is sponsored by the Montclair Art Museum and is part of its sixth annual Independent Film Makers series. Tickets at the theater, 486 Bloomfield Avenue, are $8, or $5 for students and the elderly. 

Information: (201) 746-5555.
ECONOMIC CONFERENCE 

 "World Economic Outlook for the 1990's: The U.S. and New Jersey" will be the topic of a daylong conference on Tuesday at the Bishop Dougherty Student Center at Seton Hall University on South Orange Avenue in South Orange. 

The conference will deal with the economic impact of the unification of Germany, the crisis in Kuwait coupled with the continuing tensions in the Middle East and the emergence of the European Economic Community and the prospects of a Common Market for the Americas. Business prospects in Asia, particularly in Japan, will be the subject of a panel discussion. The conference will be held from 9 A.M. to 4:30 P.M., with registration beginning at 8:30 A.M.

William Niskanen, director of the Cato Institute and a former member of the Council of Economic Advisers to President Ronald Reagan, will be the keynote speaker at 9 A.M. He is the author of "Reaganomics: An Insider's Account of the Policies and People," among other books. He was a founder of the National Tax Limitation Committee and has contributed to the drafting of tax limitation legislations. 

The conference is sponsored by the New Jersey Chamber of Commerce in cooperation with the W. Paul Stillman School of Business at Seton Hall University.

Registration is $75 at the door. Information: (201) 761-9220. 
IRISH CELEBRATION 

 The Celtic Theater of Seton Hall University will present poems and music of Ireland this afternoon in Montclair at a celebration of the life of Michael L. Delahunty, which will start the establishment of a foundation to support an Irish heritage collection in his name at the Montclair Public Library.

Mr. Delahunty died on Dec. 1 at the age of 62, a month before he was to be sworn in to an elected term as an Essex County Freeholder at Large on New Year's Day. He was appointed to that office on Jan. 1, 1990, to complete the term vacated by Tom Giblin, who was elected Essex County Surrogate.

Mr. Delahunty had also served as Public Safety Commissioner of Montclair from 1976 to 1980, overseeing the Police, Fire and Civil Defense Departments. He was active in many civic, cultural and charitable organizations.

The event will be held from 2:30 to 5:30 P.M. at the Montclair Women's Club, 82 Union Street. Admission is free. 

Information: (201) 744-9552 or (201) 783-7344.

THE VIEW FROM: THE COUNCIL FOR THE ARTS;
Making Culture a Priority
WHEN the Council for the Arts began 25 years ago, it was a fledgling group of prominent Westchester residents interested in building a Lincoln Center-type institution in the county. Its first grant, from the State Council for the Arts, was for $1,500. Now the council operates on a yearly budget of $2 million, with a staff of 14, and is the largest private nonprofit arts council in the state.

The council will celebrate its 25th year on Saturday with "Reflections," a sold-out gala to be held at Arrowwood in Rye Brook. Its organizers say it will outshine all the previous benefits and, with a goal of $140,000, the event is "the largest ever," said Melinda Bena, one of its chairwomen.

In the Region: Long Island;
In Long Beach, It's 'Let's Make a Deal'
AFTER almost a decade of intense development, major residential building in the city of Long Beach has come to a virtual standstill. "Developers are still putting up one- and two-family houses, but except for that, we're in a holding pattern right now," said City Manager Edwin L. Eaton. 

Development was so frenzied that new zoning to limit density was adopted three years. But now, oceanfront projects that came on the market after sales began to soften are encountering such meager demand that developers are turning to rentals, auctions and other marketing strategies to fill empty units. Others are even building affordable housing on oceanfront sites while waiting for a stronger market.

Connecticut Q&A;: Janice Weinberg;
Job-Hunting Skills for Tough Times
FINDING a job, or changing careers, is usually challenging, but especially so during the kind of slowdown that Connecticut is now experiencing. In her book "How To Win the Job You Really Want," published last year, Janice Weinberg, a career consultant, includes a section on "Looking for a Job During a Recession."

For 14 years, Ms. Weinberg worked in programming and information systems, sales, marketing and strategic planning for companies like I.B.M. and General Electric. She also served as a consultant and taught at the General Electric Management Development Institute. In 1979, she founded Career Solutions, a company that provides career assistance to individuals and outplacement services to corporations.

NORTHEAST NOTEBOOK: Lee, Mass.;
1885 School Is Now an Inn
A FORMER Roman Catholic elementary school building in this Berkshire Mountain community has been moved and converted into the Chambery Inn, whose guest suites incorporate the blackboards and chalk trays of the onetime classrooms. 

The frame structure, built in 1885 as St. Mary's School, was the first parochial school in Berkshire County and the first in the United States operated by the Sisters of St. Joseph of Chambery, a French order that later established other American schools.

From 1957 to 1977, after St. Mary's parish built a new elementary school, this town of about 6,000 residents used the 7,500-square-foot building for a public kindergarten. In 1988, when the local parish planned to demolish the building, Joseph M. Toole bought it for $1 

"I didn't have any plans for it but I didn't want to see it torn down," said Mr. Toole, a Lee native who attended kindergarten in the building. 

He had the 300-ton building moved to a nearby half-acre lot on the corner of Main and Elm Streets next to his family's insurance agency.

Early this year, after unsuccessfully trying to market the building for office space, Mr. Toole decided to make it an inn, which he now operates full time. He estimated the combined cost of the move, site work, remodeling and furnishings at $750,000. 

Mr. Toole said he had borne two-thirds of the costs and had borrowed the rest.

The nine-room inn is on a slight slope and Mr. Toole had a new foundation and lower floor built. There are guest rooms and a dining area on the lower floor. The classrooms in the upper two stories were remodeled as guest suites, with partitions added for closets and bathrooms. Gas fireplaces were also installed. 

Blackboards, tin ceilings, fixtures and other structural features have been retained and no walls were removed, said Mr. Toole, who calls himself a "purist."

The inn opened in October, with rates ranging from $60 to $120 in winter and $70 to $175 in summer. Mr. Toole said that his occupancy rate last month was 45 percent but that he was not worried about undertaking the venture in an economic downturn because of the inn's unusual qualities. 

While the town, which straddles exit 2 of the Massachusetts Turnpike, is a Berkshire County tourist destination and has other inns and visitor-oriented businesses, its economy relies more than other towns nearby -- such as Stockbridge -- on industry and local business.

The Chambery Inn is a mile north of exit 2 across the street from a converted train station that was remodeled in the late 1970's into the Sullivan Station restaurant.

In the Region: Long Island;
Building New Houses Closer to the Hubs
NEXT month, Peter and Debra Savage will move into their first home, a two-bedroom town house in an Islip development called Hansen's Landing. One of the attractions, and a major reason for buying their $129,990 house, Mr. Savage said, is the "super convenient location." 

The Long Island Rail Road station is a block and a half away and shopping along Montauk Highway, Islip's Main Street, is two blocks away.

L.I. Retailers Are Bracing For Year's Crucial Month
WITH the holiday shopping season in full swing, major Long Island retailers are reporting that business appears good despite a regional economic downturn and that the important post-Thanksgiving weekend was generally better than they had expected.

The retailers and other analysts said shoppers appeared especially conscious of prices. Some retailers predicted a shift in gift buying toward practical items. Video games, for example, may give way to sweaters, they said.

Your Own Account;
When a Small Business Stumbles
FOUR years ago, when Dr. Stephen I. Hudis of Manhattan bought the practice of a dentist who was retiring, he acquired a solid client base, but plenty of business problems. Patients didn't pay when they had the work done, so he was carrying about $70,000 a month in accounts receivable. Then, when the New York economy turned sour after the 1987 stock market crash, people started to put off major dental work, stretch out the time between routine exams and further delay payments. "That has really affected my business," he said.

Nothing he had learned in dental school helped. "They teach you nothing about business," said Dr. Hudis, who has been practicing for 11 years. "I felt overwhelmed." On the recommendation of a friend, he turned to Lynn Garber, a small-business consultant in Yardley, Pa., who urged him to revamp his system so that bills went out sooner and clients were asked to pay as dental work was done. Within months, he was getting paid for business on the books and doing marketing -- such as sending out champagne to patients who recommended several others -- that, he believed, brought in new business.

Bush Loses the Right Wing
Not since Ronald Reagan's challenge to Gerald Ford has the Republican Party been as bitterly divided as it is today. George Bush and his minions are heading into a civil war with G.O.P. conservatives that will leave the political fields covered with blood.

President Bush has lost all his credibility with conservatives. They see his support for a constitutional amendment limiting the consecutive terms of members of Congress the way they see his lukewarm support for a flag-protection amendment: as a throw-away applause line for campaign speeches, an attempt to buy their support on the cheap.

THE VIEW FROM: WESTPORT;
Customers and Complaints Mingle on Trendy Main Street
WILLIAM CRIBARI sipped coffee in the Westport Pizzeria, stared out onto Main Street and reminisced about a town that no longer existed. The 70-year-old special officer for the Westport police said his was a Main Street that had a huge fountain in the center; Papa George's Diner, where he could order a thick hamburger; Bill's Smoke Shop for a good cigar. From his boyhood, he said, Main Street was the place where a small-town allegiance was forged. 

"I've been walking this beat for 29 years, and there've been so many changes recently, so many people you don't know, that I feel like I'm in a different town," said Officer Cribari, who was born and raised in Westport.

World Markets;
The Dollar in Germany's Future
DURING the rush to unity that culminated with the creation of a single German state on Oct. 3, the dollar's steady slide to one record low after another was largely ignored by the nation's eight stock exchanges. Investors and stock experts were focused on the potential for profits in building the Eastern Europe of the future, rather than on problems stemming from currency rate fluctuations of the moment.

In fact, the German attitude toward the mark's waxing strength against the dollar was reminiscent of a comment often heard when the dollar soared during the heyday of the Reagan Administration: a strong mark reflects a strong economy.

NATIONAL NOTEBOOK: TAMPA, FLA.;
Glut Is Feared As Offices Rise
A SURGE in office construction is under way downtown amid concern that there are not enough tenants to go around.

A $140 million convention center has just been completed; the Mack Company got a certificate of occupancy last month for its second downtown building, the 28-story Two Mack Center, and there will be a million square feet of office space in two towers to be completed in 1992.

Corporations Pitch In to Aid Communities
FROM the glass-framed boardrooms of Newark to the flower-bedecked bridges of New Brunswick, corporate leaders are playing larger roles in improving the communities they serve. They call this responsibility and say they are learning to care.

They point to a range of social and civic programs, from a child-safety project and after-school job training to neighborhood beautification and renovation assistance, as indicative of their continuing commitment to communities across New Jersey. They add that their most significant contribution remains their commitment to build and expand in New Jersey, thereby providing employment, tax revenue and stimulus for business development, all of which are of critical importance in distressed areas.

LONG ISLAND Q&A;: SOL C. CHAIKEN;
From Union Head to Business Promoter
SOL C. CHAIKIN, who was long associated with the labor movement, is now chairman and acting chief executive of the Jacob K. Javits Convention Center in Manhattan. He is also associated with the Council on Foreign Relations, Freedom House, the Trilateral Commission and the International Rescue Committee. 

Mr. Chaikin attended City College and graduated from Brooklyn Law School, in 1940. He went to work as an organizer for the International Ladies Garment Workers Union, and in 1975 was elected president of the I.L.G.W.U. In 1983, he led the first official A.F.L.-C.I.O. visit to South Africa to meet new union workers there. 

Mr. Chaikin and his wife, Rosalind, live in Great Neck. He is 73 years old this month. 

 Q. Do the closing of Pennsylvania Station by the Long Island Rail Road at certain hours and reports of high crime in New York deter convention business?

A. By and large, the people who come here from out of town as exhibitors, or as customers planning to place orders, stay in midtown hotels. They also have a shuttle bus that takes them from their hotel to the center and back, and they dine in midtown restaurants, to which they take a cab. In short, they are not in harm's way. Crime that is committed within the immediate area of midtown hotels, I believe, is at a minimum. New York is not the most crime-ridden city in America, given the population. It is 17th or 18th. 

The only problem with crime in New York is the publicity attached to these crimes. If a person in New York is raped or mugged, it's all over the country. 

Q. A promotional film about Jacksonville, Fla., spoke glowingly about a warm climate, nonunion work forces, wonderful recreational facilities and a low crime rate. Why would any exhibitor want to set up shop in New York?

A. New York is the premier city in the universe. No nonsense about it. It is the center of commerce. Not for every single product that is produced in the United States, but we are the biggest, strongest market overall. 

Q. What effect will the 1992 Democratic convention have on the Javits Center?

A. If use of the center by the Democrats adds to the revenue in the sense that we are able to sell the available space that is not being used by them, and if we don't lose any previous commitments because of the Democrats, it will add to our revenue. We will build the Democrats into and on top of what we normally expect at the center.

Q. Is the center giving the Democrats preferential treatment?

A. Only in the sense that we have squeezed some of our contractual tenants into booking less space or into taking different space at the center. The center is big enough to provide the space that the Democrats say they need. 

Q. Has any exhibitor balked at coming to New York because of the convention?

A. We did have one major exhibitor book the center and then call to cancel. They were afraid of not having enough hotel rooms, cabs and various creature comforts. I told them we would take care of everything, and they decided to stay with the center. But they were within inches of going to Miami or Los Angeles.

 Q. The National Horse Show recently left Madison Square Garden for the Byrne Arena in the Meadowlands. Although the Javits Center could not be host for the show, do suburban centers like the Nassau Veterans Coliseum pose a threat to it?

A. Every facility is competition, but I don't see the coliseum or the Meadowlands as meaningful competition. We don't have every show every year. Some shows come once every other year, others every three years. Other shows must have exposure to various parts of the country, and they come to the Javits Center every five or six years.

On the other hand, we have shows that come twice a year and every year. It depends on the industry and product and how important New York is to their scheme of things. 

Q. Do you believe that Mayor David N. Dinkins has lost credibility with the labor movement?

A. The present labor discussions are long and arduous, and are being held under extraordinarily difficult circumstances. After a settlement has been reached, the parties will turn to the other major problems. 

There still will be crime in the streets and a lessening of economic activity, and you will still have a million people on welfare and the question of the infrastructure. 

 Q. Could you compare Edward I. Koch with Mr. Dinkins?

A. I don't like to compare. When Koch took over for Beame, there was major support for the cities and the economy was on the upturn. Wall Street, real estate and the legal profession all were booming and created good-paying jobs. Those people paid taxes.

Dinkins took over when the Federal Government was cutting back aid and the economy was weakening. You judge a mayor and his or her effectiveness by the conditions under which they have to govern. 

Q. What is your opinion of The Daily News strike?

A. You would have to be a party to the negotiations and have an intimate knowledge of the conditions under which The News management operates and under which the workers work. Every time a situation like this happens, it is really a failure of the labor-management relationship. Each party in the case has failed somehow in creating the circumstances under which you could have rational conversation and under which a settlement can be pursued. 

Q. Did the strike surprise you?

A. I was aware that The Daily News was preparing for a strike and that the unions would seek to avoid a strike to the fullest extent possible. The fact that they struck means that their patience ran out, or they could not just be immobile in the face of some activities that management engaged in. 

Q. Will or should Gov. Mario M. Cuomo run for President?

A. Under certain conditions, I can see him running, and under other conditions I can see him grateful for the cup having passed his lips. 

If the beginning of this recession becomes deeper and tougher, if our presence in the Persian Gulf becomes inconclusive and if the President is perceived as being indecisive, Mario Cuomo has the obligation to the citizens and the Democratic party to seriously consider becoming a contender and having his voice heard throughout the country as a contender. 

Q. Do you favor foreign automobile production in the United States?

A. You have very few choices that are acceptable. The best choice is for a Japanese company to open in America and employ American workers. I believe those workers would be a little better off if they were represented by a union like the United Auto Workers. The big deal is that they are employing Americans. 

If I had my druthers, I would prefer that we produce all of the cars made in America, meaning American corporations. 

 Q. How did you become chief executive at the Javits center? 

A. My position as chief executive and president came about as basically an accident. Jack Krumpe, the previous chief executive, made it clear that he would come on board for three years. The governing board was aware of this. 

Jack left a much-higher-paying job at Gulf & Western to come to the center. Last April, he told me it was time for him to leave. It is up to the board of directors to elect the chief executive.

 Q. Should the Javits Center expand?

A. I believe we should expand. But I will not name a particular area, because the property owners read that and they usually raise their selling price.

Q. How could you justify expansion in a period of economic downturn?

A. Easily. It takes five years from conception to the actual building of any project of meaningful proportions. Why not plan today while business is in a downward spiral, and may get worse next year, to do something that will be in operation five years from now?

 Q. How is the Javits Center operated?

A. We are subsidized by the state, which pays both the interest and amortization on the bonds and a couple of million dollars a year to meet our operating expenses. The center is run like a private business. We try not to spend more money than our anticipated revenue.

But we have overhead expenses that we cannot cut back on. Sometimes those expenses exceed our revenues. We then must look to the Governor and the Legislature to make good on the deficit. Last year our subsidy was $1.5 million. But we did not spend it all and we used what was left for this year's deficit. We will survive until business picks up, or until we get smarter and tougher and cut some of our expenses. 

The center is operated as an entrepreneurial activity. We are in business. New York State does not have to compete with anybody. If the state needs to build a road, it builds one. If the Long Island Expressway has potholes, a driver still must use it.

 Q. Is the dream of suburban living a memory?

A. Originally, Long Island promised open space, a wide choice of communities and competitive prices in many areas. The population has grown on Long Island and in the rest of the United States. 

I remember when we had 180 million people. Now it is 260 million. They have to live somewhere, and they can't all live in Montana. If I had the money, I might consider living in Montana and seeing my grandchildren and children in the East once a month. 

Q. You have a home in Florida. There appears to be a migration to Florida by young Long Islanders. What is the reason for this?

A. Florida is growing and, hopefully, these people have the needed skills and entrepreneurial spirit. Frankly, I have not been in Florida longer than two weeks at a time. Hopefully, when my stint at the Javits Center is over, I can spend at least a month down there.

Q. Has the quality of life changed since you moved to Great Neck in 1959?

A. I would say no. But I've traveled around Long Island and I'm aware that some communities are troubled by homelessness and unemployment. But not every suburb has the same problems. As the suburbs age and the population grows, they begin to outgrow their infrastructure. Great Neck used to be fairly quiet. You could go into town on a Saturday and shop at your leisure. Now, you have to maneuver for a parking space and so forth. The stores are suburban and do a good business, although I have recently noticed some vacant storefronts. 

Q. Do you still advise the labor movement?

A. Not really. I could have stayed with the I.L.G.W.U. until the present, if I had wanted to. There was little opposition to my remaining as president of the union, if I so desired. I pledged to my family when I was 68 that I would not be involved with any more union activity.

Q. What would you like your legacy to be at the Javits Center?

A. I think I have run the center just as capably as the so-called professional executive. Perhaps I'll be remembered as doing a little better than some.
Peat Says It Will Cut 300 Partners

Real Estate;
Downturn in Boston Area Is Expanding to Route 495
THE economic downturn that has slowed commercial real estate development in downtown Boston has now spread into areas far beyond the city and is especially acute along Route 495, the beltway that sweeps in a 115-mile arc from Cape Cod to Newburyport, Mass., just below the New Hampshire border.

Since the first sections of Route 495 opened in the 1970's, this roadway has been extended to pass through 43 cities and towns and run within 25 miles of Providence, R.I., and Worcester, Mass. It lies within 40 miles of Boston, connected by the Massachusetts Turnpike and Route 9.
